**Canadian French** ([French](/wiki/French_language "French language"): *français canadien*, [[fʁãˈsɛ kanaˈd͡zjɛ̃]](/wiki/Help:IPA "Help:IPA")) is the [French language](/wiki/French_language "French language") as it is spoken in Canada. It includes [multiple varieties](/wiki/Varieties_of_French#Canada "Varieties of French"), the most prominent of which is Québécois ([Quebec French](/wiki/Quebec_French "Quebec French")). Formerly *Canadian French* referred solely to Quebec French and the closely related varieties of [Ontario](/wiki/Ontario "Ontario") ([Franco-Ontarian](/wiki/Franco-Ontarian "Franco-Ontarian")) and [Western Canada](/wiki/Western_Canada "Western Canada")—in contrast with [Acadian French](/wiki/Acadian_French "Acadian French"), which is spoken by [Acadians](/wiki/Acadians "Acadians") in [New Brunswick](/wiki/New_Brunswick "New Brunswick") (including the [Chiac](/wiki/Chiac "Chiac") [dialect](/wiki/Dialect "Dialect")) and some areas of [Nova Scotia](/wiki/Nova_Scotia "Nova Scotia") (including the dialect [St. Marys Bay French](/wiki/St._Marys_Bay_French "St. Marys Bay French")), [Prince Edward Island](/wiki/Prince_Edward_Island "Prince Edward Island") and [Newfoundland & Labrador](/wiki/Newfoundland_and_Labrador "Newfoundland and Labrador") (where [Newfoundland French](/wiki/Newfoundland_French "Newfoundland French") is also spoken).

## Dialects and varieties

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Canadian_French&action=edit&section=1 "Edit section: Dialects and varieties")]

*[Quebec French](/wiki/Quebec_French "Quebec French")* is spoken in Quebec. Closely related varieties are spoken by Francophone communities in Ontario, Western Canada and the [New England](/wiki/New_England "New England") region of the United States, differing only from Quebec French primarily by their greater [linguistic conservatism](/wiki/Conservative_and_innovative_language "Conservative and innovative language"). The term *Laurentian French* has limited applications as a collective label for all these varieties, and *Quebec French* has also been used for the entire dialect group. The overwhelming majority of [francophone Canadians](/wiki/Francophone_Canadians "Francophone Canadians") speak this dialect.

*[Acadian French](/wiki/Acadian_French "Acadian French")* is spoken by over 350,000 Acadians in parts of the [Maritime provinces](/wiki/The_Maritimes "The Maritimes"), Newfoundland, the [Magdalen Islands](/wiki/Magdalen_Islands "Magdalen Islands"), the [Lower North Shore](/wiki/C%C3%B4te-Nord "Côte-Nord") and the [Gaspé Peninsula](/wiki/Gasp%C3%A9_Peninsula "Gaspé Peninsula").[[4]](#cite_note-4) [St. Marys Bay French](/wiki/St._Marys_Bay_French "St. Marys Bay French") is a variety of Acadian French spoken in Nova Scotia.

*[Brayon French](/wiki/Brayon "Brayon")* is spoken in [Madawaska County](/wiki/Madawaska_County "Madawaska County"), New Brunswick, and, to a lesser extent, [Aroostook County](/wiki/Aroostook_County "Aroostook County"), Maine, and [Beauce](/wiki/Beauce,_Quebec "Beauce, Quebec") of Quebec. Although superficially a [phonological](/wiki/Phonological "Phonological") descendant of Acadian French, analysis reveals it is [morphosyntactically](/wiki/Morphosyntactic "Morphosyntactic") identical to Quebec French.[[5]](#cite_note-5) It is believed to have resulted from a localized levelling of contact dialects between Québécois and Acadian [settlers](/wiki/Settler "Settler").

*[Métis French](/wiki/M%C3%A9tis_French "Métis French")* is spoken in [Manitoba](/wiki/Manitoba "Manitoba") and Western Canada by the [Métis](/wiki/M%C3%A9tis_in_Canada "Métis in Canada"), descendants of [First Nations](/wiki/First_Nations_in_Canada "First Nations in Canada") mothers and [voyageur](/wiki/Voyageurs "Voyageurs") fathers during the [fur trade](/wiki/Fur_trade "Fur trade"). Many Métis spoke [Cree](/wiki/Cree_language "Cree language") in addition to French, and over the years they developed a unique mixed language distinct from their French dialect called [Michif](/wiki/Michif_language "Michif language") by combining Métis French nouns, numerals, articles and adjectives with Cree verbs, [demonstratives](/wiki/Demonstrative "Demonstrative"), [postpositions](/wiki/Postpositions "Postpositions"), [interrogatives](/wiki/Interrogatives "Interrogatives") and pronouns. Both the Michif language and the Métis dialect of French are [severely endangered](/wiki/Language_death "Language death").

*[Newfoundland French](/wiki/Newfoundland_French "Newfoundland French")* is spoken by a small population on the [Port au Port Peninsula](/wiki/Port_au_Port_Peninsula "Port au Port Peninsula") of Newfoundland. It is endangered—both Quebec French and Acadian French are now more widely spoken among Newfoundland Francophones than the distinctive peninsular dialect.

### Sub-varieties

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Canadian_French&action=edit&section=2 "Edit section: Sub-varieties")]

There are two main sub-varieties of Canadian French. *[Joual](/wiki/Joual "Joual")* is an informal variety of French spoken in working-class neighbourhoods in Quebec. *[Chiac](/wiki/Chiac "Chiac")* is a blending of Acadian French syntax and vocabulary, with numerous [lexical borrowings](/wiki/Lexical_borrowing "Lexical borrowing") from English.

## Historical usage

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Canadian_French&action=edit&section=3 "Edit section: Historical usage")]

The term "Canadian French" was formerly used to refer specifically to Quebec French and the closely related varieties of Ontario and Western Canada descended from it.[[6]](#cite_note-6) This is presumably because Canada and Acadia were distinct parts of [New France](/wiki/New_France "New France"), and also of [British North America](/wiki/British_North_America "British North America"), until 1867. The term is no longer usually deemed to exclude Acadian French.

[Phylogenetically](/wiki/Phylogenetics "Phylogenetics"), Quebec French, Métis French and Brayon French are representatives of [koiné](/wiki/Koin%C3%A9_language "Koiné language") French in the Americas whereas Acadian French, Cajun French, and Newfoundland French are derivatives of non-koiné local dialects in France.[*[which?](/wiki/Wikipedia:Avoid_weasel_words "Wikipedia:Avoid weasel words")*][[7]](#cite_note-7)

The term [anglicism](/wiki/Anglicism "Anglicism") (*anglicisme*) is related to the linguistic concepts of [loanwords](/wiki/Loanwords "Loanwords"), [barbarism](/wiki/Barbarism_(linguistics) "Barbarism (linguistics)"), [diglossia](/wiki/Diglossia "Diglossia"), and the [macaronic](/wiki/Macaronic "Macaronic") mixture of the French and English languages.

According to some, French spoken in Canada includes many anglicisms. The "*Banque de dépannage linguistique*" (Language Troubleshooting Database) by the [Office québécois de la langue française](/wiki/Office_qu%C3%A9b%C3%A9cois_de_la_langue_fran%C3%A7aise "Office québécois de la langue française")[[8]](#cite_note-8) distinguishes between different kinds of anglicisms:[[9]](#cite_note-9)

* Complete anglicisms are words or groups of [loan words](/wiki/Loan_words "Loan words") from the English language. The form is often exactly the same as in English (e.g., "glamour", "short", and "sweet"), but sometimes there is a slight adjustment to the French language (e.g., "*drabe*", which comes from the English word "drab").
* Hybrid anglicisms are new words, formed by the addition of a French element to an English word. This element (a [suffix](/wiki/Suffix "Suffix"), for instance) sometimes replaces a similar element of the English word. "*Booster*" is an example of hybrid anglicism; it is made up of the English verb "to boost", to which the French suffix –er is added.
* Semantic anglicisms are French words used in a sense which exists in English but not in French. Examples include *ajourner* ("postpone") in the sense of "to have a break", *pathétique* in the sense of "miserable" or "pitiful", *plancher* ("floor/surface") in the sense of "floor" (level of a building), and *préjudice* ("harm/injury") in the sense of "(unfavorable) opinion".
* Syntactic anglicisms are those relating to the word order of a [sentence](/wiki/Sentence_(linguistics) "Sentence (linguistics)") and the use of [prepositions](/wiki/Preposition_and_postposition "Preposition and postposition") and [conjunctions](/wiki/Conjunction_(grammar) "Conjunction (grammar)"). The expression "*un bon dix minutes*" ("a good ten minutes"), for instance, comes from the English language; the more conventional French wording would be "*dix bonnes minutes*". The use of the preposition *pour* ("for") after the verbs *demander* ("ask [for]") and *chercher* ("search/look [for]") is also a syntactic anglicism.
* Morphological anglicisms are literal translations (or *[calques](/wiki/Calques "Calques")*) of the English forms. With this kind of borrowing, every element comes from French, but what results from it as a whole reproduces, completely or partly, the image transmitted in English. The word *technicalité*, for instance, is formed under English influence and does not exist in standard French (which would instead use the phrasing "*détail technique*"). *À l'année longue* ("all year long"), *appel conférence* ("conference call"), and *prix de liste* ("list price") are other morphological examples of anglicisms.
* Finally, sentencial anglicisms are loan [idioms](/wiki/Idiom "Idiom") peculiar to the English language. The expressions *ajouter l'insulte à l'injure* ("[add insult to injury](https://en.wiktionary.org/wiki/add_insult_to_injury "wiktionary:add insult to injury")") and *sonner une cloche* ("[ring a bell](https://en.wiktionary.org/wiki/ring_a_bell "wiktionary:ring a bell")") are sentencial anglicisms.

Academic, [colloquial](/wiki/Colloquialism "Colloquialism"), and [pejorative](/wiki/Pejorative "Pejorative") terms are used in Canada to refer to the [vernacular](/wiki/Vernacular "Vernacular"). Examples are *des "sabirisation"* (from *sabir*, "[pidgin](/wiki/Pidgin "Pidgin")"), [Franglais](/wiki/Franglais "Franglais"), [Français québécois](/wiki/Fran%C3%A7ais_qu%C3%A9b%C3%A9cois "Français québécois"), and Canadian French.
